1: halleluyah — hodu lYHWH ki-tov ki leolam chasdo — the liturgical refrain shared with Psalms 107:1; Psalms 118:1; Psalms 136:1 (where it appears 26 times). In Ps 106 it frames a catalog of failures — the refrain's stability is precisely the answer to the instability of the history it introduces. The community praises despite the record of betrayal because YHWH's chesed endures leolam (forever).
2: mi-ymallel gevurot YHWH — malal Pi. (to speak out fully, declare) in interrogative form: who is able? The question is rhetorical — no one can fully recount God's mighty acts. yashmi kol-tehillato (make heard all his praise) — the incompleteness of any human doxology is itself a doxological acknowledgment: God exceeds all praise.
3: ashre shomre mishpat osse tzedaqah bkhol-et — the ashre formula (blessed/happy) introduces the ethical condition against which the following confession of failure will be measured. bkhol-et (at every time) is the standard the nation chronically fails — the psalm will document how the 'every time' became 'almost never.' The beatitude sets up the tragic irony of vv6-46.
4: zkhoreni YHWH birtzon amekha — a sudden shift to first-person singular, unique in this communal psalm. The individual voice lodges a personal petition within the corporate lament. ratzon (favor/pleasure/goodwill) is the positive divine disposition toward the elect: the petitioner asks to be included in that which God already intends for his people.
5: lirot btvuvat bhirekhah — bachir (chosen one) continues the election theme from Ps 105. The three purposes (lirot, lsimchah, lhithallel) — to see, to share joy, to boast/praise with the inheritance — show that individual salvation is inseparable from communal participation in God's purposes. To be saved is to be incorporated into the worshiping community.
6: chatanu im-avotenu — the three-verb confession: chata (miss the mark/sin), avah (bend/act crookedly, iniquity), rasha (act wickedly) — the moral spectrum from inadvertent failure to deliberate rebellion. im-avotenu (along with/together with our fathers) — the solidarity is not merely historical comparison but genuine corporate identification: we sinned, they sinned, the community is one continuous body.
7: lo-hiskilu niphleotekhah — hiskil (Hiphil of sakal, give attention to/understand) — the failure was cognitive as well as volitional. Ps 105 commanded sihu (reflect) on the wonders; the fathers at the sea did not. lo-zakhru (did not remember) — the antithesis of zachar that drove Ps 105. wayyamru al-yam-suph (they rebelled at the Red Sea) — the rebellion occurred at the very site of the miracle.
8: wayyoshiem lmaan shmo — the decisive theological pivot: God's motivation is not Israel's merit but his own name/reputation. lmaan shmo (for the sake of his name) is the most common stated reason for divine action toward rebellious Israel (Ezekiel 20:9, 14, 22; Psalms 36:22). The name is God's character made public; to act for his name's sake is to act from who he is, not from who Israel is.
9: wayyighar byam-suph — gaar (to rebuke/threaten) is the term for God's command to chaos waters (Psalms 104:7; Nahum 1:4). At the Red Sea, God treats the sea as a chaos power to be subdued — the Exodus has cosmic dimensions. wayyhalekh bacharvot (he walked through the dry places) — halakh of the sea itself: the water that was obstacle becomes pathway.
10: wayyoshiem miyad soneh — sane (hating enemy) and gaal (to redeem as kinsman) in the same verse: the rescue from enemy is framed in family-law terms. goel (kinsman redeemer) acts when a family member is enslaved or dispossessed — God acts for Israel as their nearest relative, with full legal standing.
11: wayykhasu-mayim tzareyhem — kasah (to cover/overwhelm) with water: the army that tried to cover the Israelites in slavery is itself covered by the sea. lo-notar meyhem echad (not one of them remained) — the totality of judgment echoes Exodus 14:28. The same waters that carried Israel become the tomb of their enemies.
12: wayyaaminu bidhvarayw wayyashiru tehillato — the moment of faith and song (Exodus 14:31-15:1). aman Hiphil (to trust, believe, rely on) — the Song of the Sea is Israel's high-water mark of faith in the Pentateuch. The tragedy is that vv13ff. show how quickly that faith collapsed. The Song is not the culmination of a stable faith but a peak before rapid descent.
13: meheru shakhchu maasoyw — meher (quickly, with haste) attached to shakhah (forget) is a devastating indictment: the forgetting was not gradual but precipitous. lo-chakku leatzato (they did not wait for his counsel) — chakah (to wait patiently, tarry) is the discipline they lacked; the wilderness tested precisely this. Impatience and forgetting are twin roots of rebellion.
14: wayyitaou taavah bamidbar — avah intensified as noun-verb pair (wayyitaou taavah) — an internal accusative construction: they craved a craving. The place Kibroth-hattaavah (Numbers 11:34: graves of craving) memorializes this. nasah el biyyshimon (tested God in the wasteland) — the wilderness as a proving ground cuts both ways: God tests Israel, and Israel improperly tests God.
15: wayyitten lahem sheellatam — God grants the demand (sheelah, request). wayyshallach razown bnaphsham — razown (leanness/wasting) is the terrible gift inside the granted wish: the quail comes with a wasting sickness in their beings (nephesh). God's judgment here is a 'yes' — the most fearsome divine response to impatient prayer.
16: wayyqanneu lmoshe bamachaneh — qanah (to be jealous/zealous) for the priesthood of Moses and Aaron. The irony runs deep: Phinehas will later demonstrate righteous qinna (zeal) in v30, reversing the selfish jealousy of v16. qedosh YHWH (the holy one of the LORD) — Aaron's title; to envy him is to challenge the divine order of holiness.
17: tiptach-eretz watibbela datan — the earth opened (patach) and swallowed (bala): judgment takes the form of the earth consuming those who rebelled against the LORD's appointed leader (Numbers 16:30-33). The naming of Dathan without Korah follows a tradition also seen in Deuteronomy 11:6 — Korah's line survived (Numbers 26:11) and composed psalms.
18: wattivaar-esh beadatam — baar (to burn/blaze) — fire from the LORD consumed 250 men who offered incense (Numbers 16:35). The edah (congregation/company) destroyed by fire stands as a warning against usurping the priestly office; fire that purifies the altar consumes those who approach it without authorization.
19: yaasu-egel bchorev — the Golden Calf (Exod 32) is located at Chorev (Deuteronomic name for Sinai), emphasizing that it occurred at the mountain of the law itself — not in absence of revelation but in the very shadow of it. wayyishtachavu lmassechah (they bowed down to a cast/molten image) — massechah (cast metal image) denotes the manufacturing process: they made what they worshiped.
20: wayyamiru et-kevodam btavnit shor ochel esev — mur (to exchange/substitute, commercial barter term) makes the trade explicit: kavod (glory, the divine Presence) was exchanged for a grass-eating ox. The grotesque specificity — a working animal — measures the magnitude of the descent. Jeremiah 2:11 applies this language to all of Israel's idolatry.
21: shakhchu el moshiam — moshia (Savior, Hiphil participle of yasha) — the title for Israel's personal divine Deliverer is forgotten within weeks of the Exodus events. The verb shakhah (forget) — the counterpoint to zachar — indicts the heart's capacity for self-erasure of even the most extraordinary experiences.
22: niphleot beretz-cham noraot al-yam-suph — niphleot (wonders, Niphal participle of palah) and noraot (fearsome acts, Niphal participle of yare) frame the Red Sea crossing from two angles: astonishing and awe-inducing. These are the precise acts just forgotten in v21 — the forgetting is not of distant abstractions but of recent overwhelming experience.
23: lule moshe vchiro amad babedek — bedeq (breach in a fortification wall) appears here and Ezekiel 22:30; a technical military term for a gap through which the enemy pours. Moses stands in the breach, blocking the flow of divine wrath. lehashiv chamato (to turn his anger back) — Moses's intercession is physically imaged as plugging the gap in a wall under siege.
24: wayyimasu beretz chemdah — maas (to despise/reject) is a loaded word: used for rejecting God's kingship (1 Samuel 8:7), for rejecting the word (1 Samuel 15:23), for God's rejection of Saul (1 Samuel 16:1). To despise the eretz chemdah (the desirable/lovely land) — the object of 400 years of promise — is to reject the covenant itself. lo-heeeminu lidhvaro echoes v12's momentary faith: the faith of the Song of the Sea had not endured to the border of Canaan.
25: wayyrganu beoholeyhem — ragan (to grumble/murmur, Niphal) carries a particularly negative connotation: secret malicious muttering (Deuteronomy 1:27). The private (beoholeyhem, in their tents) nature of the complaint makes it more insidious — a conspiracy of complaint rather than open dialogue. lo shameu bqol YHWH — deafness to the divine voice is the definitive covenantal failure.
26: wayyisa yadow lahem — nasa yad (to lift the hand) is the oath-swearing gesture (Genesis 14:22; Exodus 6:8); here turned against Israel: God swears an oath of judgment (Numbers 14:28-35). The same posture that seals covenant blessing now seals covenant consequence. The structure is deeply ironic: God's oath-raising hand was first raised to swear blessing; now raised to swear judgment.
27: whhaphil zaram bagoyim ulzarotam bearatzot — naphal Hiphil (to fell/cast down) and zarah (to scatter/winnow) — the diaspora language used here anticipates the Babylonian exile by centuries. The wilderness generation's failure is the paradigm for exile; the scattering into the nations is the ultimate covenant sanction (Leviticus 26:33; Deuteronomy 4:27; Psalms 28:64).
28: wayyitzamdu lbaal peor — tzamad (to bind/yoke/attach, Niphal) is a powerful metaphor: Israel is harnessed to Baal of Peor as an ox is yoked to a plow — unable to move independently. wayyochlu zivche metim (they ate sacrifices of the dead) — metim (dead ones) refers to the dead gods who cannot speak or act; to eat their sacrificial meat is to commune with nothingness.
29: wayyakhisu bemalleleyhem — kaas (to vex/provoke to anger, cause grief) — the Baal Peor apostasy actively provokes. wayyiphrats bam hamagepah (a plague burst through among them) — parats (to burst/break through): the plague broke through the defenses — the same idiom as a military breach in a wall.
30: wayyaamod phinehas wayplallel — pallal Piel is a rare verb, cognate to mishpat (judgment): Phinehas intervened/executed judgment through the physical act of Numbers 25:7-8. He stood (amad) where Moses stood in v23's breach — both interceptions stop divine wrath, one by prayer and one by action. wattaatzar hammagepah — atzar (to restrain, shut) — the same verb used for shutting the womb (Genesis 20:18) and stopping rain (1 Kings 17:1): divine judgment is shut by righteous intervention.
31: wayyechasher low ltzedaqah ldor wador ad-olam — chashav (to reckon/count/credit) is the Genesis 15:6 verb used for Abraham's faith being credited as righteousness. The echo is deliberate: Phinehas's zealous act is credited (chashav) as righteousness in exactly the same vocabulary as the paradigmatic faith of the father of the faithful. James 2's discussion of faith and works lives in the tension between these two uses of chashav.
32: wayyaqtzipu al-me merivah — qatzaph (to become angry, provoke wrath) at the waters of Merivah (strife-waters, from riv, contend). wayyera lmoshe baavoram (it went badly for Moses because of them) — the psalmist exonerates Moses: the harm came to him because of them. Moses suffers collateral damage from Israel's rebellion, making his exclusion from Canaan a tragedy rather than a simple personal punishment.
33: ki-himru et-rucho wayyevatte bsphathayw — marar (to make bitter, Hiphil himru) is the root of Miriam's name and of Naomi's self-renaming (Ruth 1:20). The people made Moses's spirit bitter. batta (to speak rashly, hapax in this form) describes Moses's fatal speech — the words spoken in frustration at Meribah (Numbers 20:10-11) that cost him entry to the land. The sin of the tongue is the culminating effect of the people's embittering.
34: lo-hishmidu et-haamim asher amar lahem YHWH — failure to execute the cherem (devoted destruction) command. The disobedience is not presented as mercy but as defection — the nations not driven out become the source of the assimilation catalogued in vv35-39. Partial obedience plants the seeds of total corruption.
35: wayyitaarvu bagoyim wayyilmdu maaseyhem — arav (to intermingle, mix together) — Israel is blended into the nations like dye into water, losing its distinctive character. lamad (to learn) — the wrong curriculum: they learned the nations' practices (maaaseyhem) rather than teaching the nations YHWH's ways. The direction of influence is completely reversed from the original mandate.
36: wayyavdu et-atzabbeyhem wayyihyu lahem lmoqesh — atzav (idol/grievous image, from atzav, to hurt/pain) — an idol is a hurt-maker. moqesh (trap/snare) fulfills the warning of Exodus 23:33; Joshua 23:13: the gods of the nations left in place would become snares. The trajectory from intermarriage (v35) to idol service (v36) to child sacrifice (vv37-38) follows the logic of unchecked assimilation.
37: wayyizbechu et-bnehem veet-bnotehem lashedim — shedim (demons) appears only here and Deuteronomy 32:17 in the Hebrew Bible. The Akkadian cognate shedu originally denoted protective spirits; the psalmist uses the term with bitter irony — what purports to protect Israel's children is actually destroying them. Child sacrifice as the terminus of idolatry: what began as worship ends as murder.
38: wayyishpkhu dam naqi — shaphak dam (to pour out/shed blood) is the idiom for murder (Genesis 9:6; Ezekiel 22:3-4). naqi (innocent, guiltless) — children bear no guilt. wattechanneph haaretz baddamim (the land was profaned by the bloodshed) — chaneph (to be profane/polluted) describes moral defilement of the land itself; the land bears the bloodguilt and becomes unlivable (Numbers 35:33-34).
39: wayyitmeu bmaaseyhem wayyiznu bmalleleyhem — two defilement verbs: tame Niphal (to become ritually/morally unclean) and zanah (to act as a harlot, spiritual adultery). The Prophets consistently use marital infidelity as the metaphor for idolatry (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16; 23) — the covenant is a marriage, and idolatry is adultery against YHWH. The defilement accumulates until the land expels its inhabitants (Leviticus 18:24-28).
40: wayyichar-aph YHWH baamow wayyetaev et-nachalatow — charah aph (his nose burned) is the standard Hebrew idiom for burning anger: the flared nostrils of rage. taav Hiphil (to cause abhorrence, make abominable) — God comes to find his own inheritance loathsome. The emotional register here is as raw as anywhere in the Psalter: YHWH abhors his own people. The covenant love remains, but the experience of covenant life has become revolting to both parties.
41: wayyitnem byad-goyim — the defeat formula: to be given into the hand of enemies is the covenantal curse language of Deuteronomy 28:25, Judges 2:14, Psalms 6:1. wayyimshlu vahem soneyhem (those who hated them ruled over them) — sane (hate, the same root as v10's persecutors at the Red Sea) — the deliverer from haters has now handed them to haters. The cycle is vicious and deliberate.
42: wayyilchatzum oyveyhem wayyikanaou tachat yadam — lachatz (to oppress/crush) is Egypt's verb (Exodus 3:9): Israel now suffers the Egyptian experience from its neighbors. kanah Niphal (to be humbled/subdued) — the same root as knaaan (Canaan): they are brought down like Canaanites in the land promised to them. The irony is complete.
43: paamim rabboth yatzilem vhemah yamru beatzato wayyamokhu baavonam — paamim rabboth (many times): the Judges cycle compressed into three words. makhakh (to sink down, collapse, be brought low) in the final clause is the terminus of the cycle: sin then servitude then cry then rescue then sin then collapse. baavonam (in/through their iniquity) — iniquity is both the cause and the weight that collapses them.
44: wayyar batzar lahem bshomeo et-rinnatam — the turning point: God sees (raah) and hears (shamaa). rinnah here denotes the distress-cry (contrast v43's joyful rinnah at departure from Egypt). The same ringing-shout word that described exultant departure is now the anguished cry of the oppressed. God responds to both registers of rinnah.
45: wayyizchor lahem britow wayyinachem krob chasadayw — the psalm's climactic verse. zachar brit (remember the covenant) — the same action that opened Ps 105's recital (v8) now closes Ps 106's lament: God's memory of the covenant is the one stable anchor in the entire turbulent history. nacham Niphal (to be moved with compassion, to relent) — the divine relenting is motivated by krob chasadayw (the abundance/greatness of his steadfast loves) — the plural chasadim denotes the fullness of covenantal faithfulness, the accumulated weight of every promise.
46: wayyitten otam lrachamim liphne kol-shovehem — rachamim (compassion, womb-mercy, plural of rechem) — God gives them to the compassion of their captors. The term rachamim, denoting the tenderest form of love (maternal womb-mercy), appears in the most unexpected place: the hearts of enemy captors. God can make oppressors show mercy (cf. Genesis 39:21 — same construction for Joseph in prison). The exile is softened from within by the same rachamim that drives YHWH himself.
47: hoshienu YHWH elohenu wqabtzenu min-hagoyim — the psalm's climactic prayer: hoshia (save) and qabatz (gather/assemble) — the two great exile-reversal verbs (cf. Deuteronomy 30:3-4; Isaiah 43:5; Psalms 54:7). The purpose stated: lhodot lshem qodshekhah (to give thanks to your holy name) — the goal of ingathering is not national restoration for its own sake but restored worship. The same hodu that opened Ps 105 (v1) is the telos of the prayer here: the cycle of praise continues.
48: barukh YHWH elohey yisrael min-haolam vad haolam — the Book IV doxology, closing Pss 90-106. vaamar kol-haam amen — the congregational response amen (truly, so be it — from aman, the same root as faith/trust in v12 and Genesis 15:6) is the community's ratification of all that has been recounted. The paired Pss 105-106 present the single covenant story from both sides — divine faithfulness and human failure — and the doxology pronounces them together as ground for blessing. halleluyah closes both psalms as the only adequate response to the whole.
